coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as novel and green nano-materials for the visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of cationic dye
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Molecular Catalysis A: Chemical
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Roushani, Mahmoud
Mavaei, Maryamosadat
Rajabi, Hamid Reza
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we have introduced a novel property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as efficient nano-materials for degradation of organic pollutant dyes based on the photocatalytic behavior of GQDs under visible light irradiation. GQD samples were synthesized directly through pyrolyzing citric acid method. The synthesized GQDs were characterized by various techniques including colorimetry, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and UV–vis absorption, Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and zeta potential measurements. In this study, a cationic dye (i.e., New Fuchsin) was chosen as a model molecule to investigate catalytic behavior of the prepared GQDs as green nanomaterials. The influence of experimental parameters such as pH of the dye solution, contacting time, dosage of GQDs, and initial concentration of NF dye on the degradation efficiency of GQD were studied. The possible mechanisms of degradation of NF based on GQDs under visible light were discussed, too.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Degradation
New fuchsin
Visible light irradiation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of initial dye concentration on degradation efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the proposed mechanism for degradation of NF under visible light irradiation by (a) photocatalytic and (b) photosensitization pathways.
(A) A typical photographical image, (B) UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of the prepared GQDs (λ ex =362nm).
Typical TEM image of GQDs prepared by directly pyrolyzing citric acid method.
The effect of pH on degradation efficiency of NF.
(A) The effect of dosage GQD in degradation of NF (10mgL−1) duration time of 110min, (B) the effect of dosage GQD on the degradation rate of NF in the presence of 15μl of GQDs in 100min.

(A) The effect of time on the decolorization efficiency of NF(20mgL−1) in the presence of 10μl of GQD (the inset figures are the decrease of the corresponding absorption spectra of the dye in the presence of GQDs), (B) change in the color of dye solution before and after addition of GQDs.
(A) The absorbance change of dye solution in their corresponding maximum wavelength in the certain time, (B,C) the effect of initial dye concentration on degradation efficiencies of NF.
Kinetic model of degradation of NF in the presence of GQD.

row.entry: New fuchsin,basic violet 2, magenta III, new magenta, fuchsin NB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pseudo-first order rate constants (k app) with corresponding correlation coefficients (R 2), maximum dye decolorization, and the maximum dye decolorization for the photocatalytic decolorization of NF in the presence of GQDs.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as novel and green nano-materials for the visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation of cationic dye

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Yasouj University, Yasouj 75918-74831, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we have introduced a novel property of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as efficient nano-materials for degradation of organic pollutant dyes based on the photocatalytic behavior of GQDs under visible light irradiation. GQD samples were synthesized directly through pyrolyzing citric acid method. The synthesized GQDs were characterized by various techniques including colorimetry, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and UV–vis absorption, Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and zeta potential measurements. In this study, a cationic dye (i.e., New Fuchsin) was chosen as a model molecule to investigate catalytic behavior of the prepared GQDs as green nanomaterials. The influence of experimental parameters such as pH of the dye solution, contacting time, dosage of GQDs, and initial concentration of NF dye on the degradation efficiency of GQD were studied. The possible mechanisms of degradation of NF based on GQDs under visible light were discussed, too.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic dyes give a potential risk to human health in excess amount [1,2]. More than 10,000 different synthetic dyes and pigments are annually produced worldwide. The annual production of dyes and pigments exceeds more than 7×105 tons of which approximately 5–15% is lost in industrial effluents [3]. The presence of even very small amounts of dyes in water can cause serious environmental problems. For example, the growth of aquatic bacteria can be reduced by light penetration into water by organic dye molecules [4]. Thus, decolorization of pollutants in water has attracted much interest in the last decades due to the large-scale production of synthetic dyes, high toxicity, slow biodegradation, and low decolorization[5]. The methods used for the removal of organic dyes and pigments from wastewaters can be divided into three main categories: (i) physical [6], (ii) chemical [7], and (iii) biological methods [8]. Photocatalytic decolorization is one of these methods which is gaining more attention because of its efficiency, versatility and convenience for the treatment of pollutants [9,10].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene sheet smaller than 100nm [11].GQDs are recently reported to be applicable in various fields such as photovoltaic devices [12], cellular imaging [13], drug carrier [13,14], and electronic and optoelectronic researches [15]. GQDs have also been used as bio-markers [16] and applied to sensitive glucose determination [16] because of their chemical stability, suitable conductivity, good biocompatibility, and low-toxicity. Also, photoexcited GQDs are very well electron acceptors and donors [17]. Moreover, GQDs can be studied as novel and efficient visible light induced nano photocatalysts due to their band gap well-corresponding with the spectrum of visible light, making it active under visible light.

Until now, various synthetic routes of fabricating GQDs have been investigated including “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches. The “top-down” methods are implemented via chemical, physical or electrochemical techniques to cut down large carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets and carbon fibers, into small pieces of GQDs. However, current “top-down” methods are still have deficiencies in strict requirement for special equipment, critical synthesis conditions, precise controlling lateral dimensions as well as obtaining high quantum yields. “Bottom-up” method is based on special organic precursor to assemble, polymeriz and carbonize to nanoscale product. Although complex, these methods allow for excellent control of the properties of the final product. However, these methods always involve boring synthetic procedures, and the special organic precursors may be difficult to be obtained [18,19].

In this paper, we present a simple “bottom-up” method for the synthesis of GQDs. The key factor of our design was the usage of a green and natural citric acid (CA) as the precursor. Our preparation method requires no elaborate equipment and no time-consuming. Water, as a green solvent used in hydrothermal synthesis, plays an important role in atom economical reactions. So our preparation method is green, fast, with their high precursor availability, low cost and the obtained GQDs have good biocompatibility, which is more suitable for the environmental applications. Most importantly, compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), GQDs are chemically inert. Thus, the potential toxicity of the intrinsic heavy-metals in semiconductor materials would not be a concern for GQDs.

This study presents a simple, safe, and efficient method for the fast degradation of pollutant dyes (i.e., new fuchsin (NF)) based on decolorization activity of GQD. GQDs were synthesized via a directly pyrolyzing citric acid method. After characterization of the prepared nanoparticles, GQD samples were applied for investigation of decolorization of NF as a pollutant dye model. Although much research has been conducted on other methods, the degradation of NF dye based on GQD under visible light irradiation has remained understudied.

New fuchsin dye was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). New fuchsin dye’s structure and characteristics are given in Table 1 . A digital pH meter, Metrohm model 632, equipped with a combined glass calomel electrode was used for the pH adjustments. Citric acid (CA) was prepared from Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany). All solutions were prepared using double distilled water.

All UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded using a VARIAN 300-Bio CARY UV–vis spectrophotometer in the range of 190–800nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were recorded with a Carry Eclipse (Agilent) Fluorescence and Phosphorescence spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations were made on a Philips CM120 transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements were performed on a Vertex 70 instrument (Vertex 70 instrument Co., Germany). To determine the charge of the GQDs surface, the zeta potential was determined from streaming potential measurements by Electro Kinetic Analyzer (EKA 1.00, Anton-Paar, Switzerland). Raman spectra were performed on an almega thermo nicolet dispersive Raman spectrometer. The degradation products were identified by mass spectrometer (5973 Network Mass Selective Detector Co. Agilent Technology).

The preparation of GQD samples was carried out by directly pyrolyzing CA according to the guidelines by Sun and associates [20]. In a typical procedure, 2.0g CA was put into a 5.0mL beaker and heated to 200°C using a heating mantle. The CA was liquated 5min later. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then to orange in 30min, implying the formation of GQDs. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 100mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. The aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained after the liquid was neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH.

In the GQD-based degradation process, an aqueous solution of NF dye with certain concentration was prepared and dispersed by ultrasonication for 10min. The initial pH of the solution was adjusted through adding either NaOH or HCl solution to the desired value. The degradation percentage of NF was studied in the presence of 10μl of GQDs as catalyst added to quartz cell containing aqueous solution of NF dye. The change in concentration of NF was monitored by UV–vis measurements at a certain time interval (at λ max =553nm). The percentage of degradation (D%) was calculated as C o − C t/Co ×100, in which C o and C t were initial concentrations of dye in the time of 0 and t, respectively.

The characterization of the prepared GQDs was performed by various techniques including colorimetry, UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, FTIR spectrophotometer, zeta potential measurements and Raman spectroscopy. A typical photographical image, UV–vis absorption and PL spectrum of the prepared GQD is shown in Fig. 1 A. As in Fig. 1B, the prepared GQD had a light yellow color with an obvious absorbance edge of about 362nm and strong emission band at 460nm. The GQD show a well-defined absorption band at 362nm, which further confirms that the sp2 clusters contained in GQD even though these sp2 clusters were doped in the sp3 matrix should be uniform in size and the surface state. When the GQD were excited at 362nm, the GQD showed a strong fluorescence peak at 460nm. GQD exhibit PL peak in the visible region, which depends on the excitation wavelength. The dependence of PL behavior could be attributed to the size effect of GQD [21]. Additionally, the prepared GQDs were also analyzed using the transmission electron microscopy. Fig. 2 presents the TEM image of a typical GQD sample prepared by directly pyrolyzing citric acid method. The GQDs had an average particle size of 15nm. The obtained results were in line with the findings of previous studies [22].

The structural changes of the citric acid during the formation of the GQDs were further characterized using FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 A and B). Comparing curve 3A with 3B in Fig. 3, the GQDs exhibit absorption of carboxyl group and hydroxyl group, showing that of nanomaterial contain COOH groups, which demonstrated the formation of GQDs. It is considerable that no characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compound (skeletal vibration of aromatic rings around 1450–1650cm−1, stretching vibration of CH in aromatic rings around 3000–3100cm−1) are contained in these FTIR spectra, indicating that no any aromatic compound is included in the obtained GQD [20].

Furthermore, measured zeta potential (−24.6mV) confirmed negatively charged character of the GQD surface. Based on the FTIR spectra and zeta potential results in this work, the GQDs showed negative charged in zeta potential measurement due to presence of numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

The Raman technique is also a powerful and non-destructive tool for distinguishing between different types of ordered and disordered bonding environments of sp2 carbon atoms and sp3-hybridised carbon. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs (Fig. 4 ) shows G and D bands at 1600cm−1 and 1377 cm−1, respectively. The ID/IG values of GQDs vary significantly depending on the preparation methods. The relative intensity of the D-band to the G-band (ID/IG) can be used to estimate the extent of structural defects. The ID/IG values of the GQD was 1.03. Such a large ratio indicates defects of the GQDs with a partially disordered crystal structure, arising from the small sp2 cluster size [23].

Fig. S1 illustrates the FTIR spectrums of the NF before degradation (a) and NF after degradation (b) process. Prior to degradation, initial NF indicated prominent bands for the C–H asymmetric stretching of CH3 at 2925cm−1, the CN central ring stretching at 1634cm−1, the CC side ring stretching at 1588cm−1, the multiple ring stretching at 1365cm−1, the CC stretching at 1017cm−1, the CC exocyclodiene at 1547cm−1, the C H out of plane bending vibrations of ring at 827cm−1. The detailed variations of IR bands during the NF degradation can be further obtained from the difference spectra in Fig. S1(b). The negative intensity reflects the scission of the special bonds in the NF, whereas the positive intensity reveals emergence of intermediate species and final products such as H2O at 1636cm−1 (OH bending vibration) and sharper one at 3324cm−1(OH stretching vibration), and CO2 at 2201cm−1 [24]. This is in accordance with previous photocatalytic degradation and oxidation studies [25–27]. The results revealed that not only the chromophore part of the NF was destroyed totally, but the structure of NF was mineralized to CO2 and water by photocatalytic degradation in the presence of GQDs.

To further confirm the occurrence of NF oxidation, the final degraded products were examined the by mass spectrometry and the results are shown in Fig. S2. In this system, the major products are corresponding to peaks (M =41), (M =43), (M =55), (M =57) and (M =69), respectively as shown in Fig. S2, the peak of NF was also extremely weak after degradation. It meant that nearly all the dye molecules were converted into inorganic compounds. The intermediates were produced through the open-ring reactions, and these intermediates could be further oxidized into small organic molecules such as organic acids and alcohols through the cleavage of the carbon reaction [28,29]. As a result, the organic dye is oxidized by GQDs and has been completely degraded into small specie.

The degradation efficiency of NF solution as a function of pH is illustrated in Fig. 5 . The degradation of NF was studied in the pH range of 3.0–11.0 in the presence of 10μl of GQDs after 110min. As shown in Fig. 5, the D% was increased by enhancing the pH of solution and reached the maximum value of >95.0 % at the pH>4.0. At low pH (pH<4.0), H+ ions can compete effectively with dye species, causing a decrease in color removal efficiency [5]. On the other hand, at low pH values, the positively charged surface GQDs are major species in the solution, thus, the repulsive forces act as major forces, then, reduced the adsorption of the dye molecules on the surface of the photocatalysts. Meanwhile, at higher pHs, the surface of GQDs was negatively charged, enhancing the positively charged dye cations [11]. However, the decolorization of dye molecules was inhibited when the pH value was very high, because the hydroxyl ions competed with dye molecules for adsorption on the surface of photocatalysts [13]. Furthermore, the GQDs commonly contain functional groups on the surfaces, such as carboxylic and epoxide groups, which lead to centers of non-radiative relaxation and radiative electron–hole (e−/p+) pair recombination [30]. GQDs at low pH contains undissociated carboxylic (COOH) groups and epoxide groups, with further increase pH, the oxygen functionalities such as carboxylic groups and phenolic OH are ionized (pH>4.0). Also, the observed decrease in D% at high pH values is because of the compression of the double layer associated with specific surface charge at high ionic strengths [31]. Therefore, the pH range of 4.0-6.0 was selected as optimum range for the next studies.

The photocatalytic degradation of NF at different quantities of catalysts for an initial concentration of 20.0mgL−1 was investigated. As indicated in Fig. 6 A, the rate of photocatalytic degradation increased with the increase of catalysts up to 5μl. To examine the dependence of the dosage performance on the degradation of NF are plotted in Fig. 6B that shows the temporal evalution of the spectral changes of NF solution (10.0mgL−1) mediated in the presence of 15μl of GQD. With the increase Dosage of GQDs, the absorption peak of NF decreased gradually, and after 100min, it became completely disappeared. The percentage degradation of dye increased exponentially with the increase in dosage of the catalyst in the presence of GQDs. This may be due to the increase in the availability of surface active sites. The effect of GQD loadings on the rate of degradation indicates that optimum dosage amount is 10μl (Fig. 6A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of initial dye concentration on degradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of NF dye was investigated at different concentrations of dye solutions (2.0–40.0mgL−1): in the presence of 10μl of GQD, at room temperature, and under visible light irradiation. Fig. 7 A demonstrates a typical decolorization result of NF (20.0mgL−1) at certain contact time intervals in the presence of GQD. A typical photographical image of the designed decolorization process of NF assisted by GQD is presented in Fig. 7B. The color of an aqueous solution containing NF dye (20ppm) disappeared after 110min in the presence of 10μl of GQD at room temperature without using UV light irradiation.

Fig. 8 shows the absorbance change of dye solution in their corresponding maximum wavelength in the certain time. As shown in Fig. 8A, the intensity of the absorption band (553nm) decreased rapidly under visible light and disappeared after about 110min, indicating the degradation of NF in the presence of 10μl of QD particles. The decolorization efficiencies of the dye in different concentrations of NF dye are shown in Fig. 8B. As shown, the decolorization of dye decreased slowly with increasing the initial NF dye concentration while concentration of GQDs was constant (see Fig. 8C). However, the observed decrease in the D% with increasing dye concentration may be due to the fact that as the initial concentration of dye increased, the NF dye molecules were adsorbed more on the surface of GQD. Thus, the active sites of GQDs were occupied by dye molecules, reducing the degradation efficiency of NF dye. On the other hand, the increase in dye molecules led to a reduction in the number of photons that reach the surface of GQD nanoparticles [8].

In the control experiments, as shown in Fig. S3, NF degraded ∼23% under irradiation of visible-light without adding any GQDs, implying that self-photolysis also contributes to the degradation of the NF. In the previous investigation [32,33] similar phenomenon has been reported, which was also ascribed to the photoexcited self-degradation of dyes. However, the photodegradation of NF was enhanced significantly in the presence of GQDs. As mentioned above, the photocatalytic degradation has been recognized as the major mechanism in degradation of NF. Photocatalytic activity enhancement was attributed to the catalyst high surface area and the photocatalytic and photosensitization pathways, which were able to act as electron traps to promote charge transfer and hence reduce recombination rate of photogenerated electron and hole.

The results obtained about kinetic studies indicated that the pseudo-first order model was found to be a better fitting model for the present heterogeneous photocatalytic experiments according to the modified Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics model [34]:
r =
d c
d t
=
k
K
dye
C
1 +
K dye
C
ln (
C
0
C t
) = k
K dye
t =
K app
t
where dC/dt was the rate of dye decolorization (mg/L min), C o and C t were the concentration of dye at time 0 and t (mg/L), respectively. Also, k was the reaction rate constant (min−1), K dye and K app were the adsorption coefficient of the dye onto the photocatalyst particle(L/mg) andapparent rate constant (min−1), respectively. The apparent rate constants(Kapp) of dye decolorization at different initial concentrations of NF were determined from the slope of the plots of ln(C o/C t) vs. time (Fig. 9 ).

The kinetic data obtained by the pseudo-first order model such as rate constants (K app), linear equation, corresponding correlation coefficients (R 2), and maximum dye degradation in the presence of GQDs are presented in Table 2 . As illustrated, K app slightly decreased with increasing the initial concentration of dye.

The possible mechanism in the graphene quantum dot based homogeneous photocatalytic process is not yet fully understood, however, following the published work [35,36,37], we hypothesize the following photoinduce degradation mechanism as illustrated in Scheme 1 and Eqs. (1)–(11); which could include both photocatalytic degradation and photosensitization degradation pathways. The visible light can be absorbed efficiently by both the GQD and the NF dye. According to the proposed pathway photocatalytic activity of the GQDs, electrons in the valence band of GQDs could be excited to the conduction band and electron–hole pairs are generated (Eq. (1)). Then the molecular oxygen (O2) near the interface photocatalyst could be quickly reduced to the superoxide radical (O2–) and hydrogen peroxide radical (OOH) by the, photogenerated electrons(Eqs. (2)–(5)), whereas the valence band holes can directly oxidize organic pollutants (i.e., the new fuchsin dye) adsorbed on the surface of catalyst (GQDs) or mineralized them indirectly through hydroxyl radicals (OH) generated by the reaction of holes and water molecules (H2O) or chemisorbed (OH−), (Eqs. (6)–(8)) [38]. In addition, in the self photosensitization pathway for the NF photo degradation in the presence of GQDs under visible light irradiation as shown in Scheme 1b. Firstly, the large surface area of GQDs can effectively adsorb NF molecules. Then, under vis-light irradiation, some photo-generated electrons can transfer from excited-state of adsorbed dye (NF) to the conduction band (CB) of GQDs. Thus, it would facilitate for the photoinduced active species (e.g. O2−, OOH, OH) to oxidize or degrade the surface absorbed organic pollutants. Subsequently, NF + readily reacts with these oxygen radicals to finally dissociate (Eqs. (9)–(11)).
(1) GQDs→GQDs (e− CB +h+ VB)
(2) GQDs (e− CB)+(O2)ads →GQDs+(O2−)ads
(3) (O2 −) ads +H+ →HO2
(4) 2HO2
→H2O2 +O2
(5) H2O2 +e− →OH− +OH
(6) GQDs (h+ VB)+(H2O)ads →GQDs+(HO)ads +(H+)ads
(7) GQDs (h+ VB)+(HO − )ads →GQDs+(HO)ads

(8) GQDs (h+ VB)+Dye ads →GQDs+(Dye +) ads
(9) Dye+hυ (vis)→Dye*
(10) Dye* +GQDs→Dye + +GQDs (e− CB)
(11) GQDs (h+ VB, OH, OOH or O2 −)+Dye→⋯→CO2 +H2O+small molecule byproducts

In this research, graphene quantum dots were prepared by directly pyrolyzing citric acid and were applied as green, efficient, and novel nanoparticles for the fast degradation of NF. The kinetic rate constants and decolorization efficiency of GQDs were found to be almost independent of the dye concentration and slightly decreased when initial concentration of NF increased. The obtained results clearly revealed that GQDs can be categorized among the novel nanophotocatalysts ever reported.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.molcata.2015.08.011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
